summary: Verified the CI Docker-build fix for the missing yarn.lock error. Static checks confirm the Dockerfile now uses `COPY yarn.lock* ./` (glob) and no longer passes `--frozen-lockfile`. .gitignore ordering is correct: line 84 `.env.*` followed by line 85 `!.env.example` and line 86 `!.env.docker.example` (negations after the ignore rule — required). Docker is not available on this runner (kubernetes container has no dockerd), so per the fallback in the review request I simulated the actual failure scenario: copied only /app/frontend/package.json to a fresh temp dir (NO yarn.lock present) and ran `yarn install --network-timeout 600000` — completed with exit 0, node_modules populated, only benign peer-dep warnings. This proves the Dockerfile's `yarn install` step will succeed on a GitHub Actions checkout that lacks yarn.lock. Regression smoke on the live preview: admin@dental.com/admin123 login → landed on `/` (no /login) → selected D2740 → tooth 14 → clinical `Fractured cusp` → clicked Generate → POST /api/generate = 200 → SHORT narrative (25 words, D2740-appropriate crown text) and LONG narrative (77 words) both rendered, URL stayed on `/`. The Dockerfile changes did not affect the live dev deployment (as expected — supervisor runs uvicorn/yarn directly, not Docker).
critical_code_review_comments: Dockerfile fix is minimal and correct — `COPY yarn.lock* ./` uses BuildKit's tolerant-glob semantics so 0-matches is fine; dropping `--frozen-lockfile` lets yarn resolve from package.json when the lockfile is absent. Recommend also committing yarn.lock to the repo so builds remain reproducible; the glob is a safety net, not a substitute.

.gitignore ordering (line 84 `.env.*`, then lines 85-86 `!.env.example` / `!.env.docker.example`) is correct — order-sensitive and the negations come AFTER the ignore rule, so the example templates get committed.

Docker build could not be exercised end-to-end here (dockerd not available in this container). The actual multi-arch GH-Actions build result will only be visible on the user's next push. If it still fails, the next candidate is BuildKit interpreting the glob differently on some runners — but the equivalent simulation (yarn install with only package.json present) succeeded cleanly.
Nit: the workflow triggers on any tag `v*.*.*` but the metadata-action `type=raw,value=latest` is gated to main pushes only — good hygiene, keeps 'latest' from ever pointing at a tag build.

success_rate.backend: N/A (no backend changes in this iteration; regression smoke via UI-integrated POST /api/generate returned 200)
success_rate.frontend: 100% (regression smoke: login + D2740 generate flow passed on preview URL)

context_for_next_testing_agent: Iteration 12 verified the Dockerfile+gitignore fix for the GitHub Actions 'yarn.lock not found' error. Static verification + a simulated `yarn install` against only package.json (no lockfile) both pass. Docker daemon is NOT available in this test container, so a real `docker build` could not be executed — that final confirmation happens on the user's GH Actions run. Regression smoke on live preview still passes (login + D2740 generate). No open issues.
rca of the issue: Root cause was two-fold in the original CI failure: (1) `/app/frontend/yarn.lock` was not present in the git-checked-out build context on the GitHub Actions runner (Emergent Save-to-GitHub excluded it, likely from a global .yarn/* or overly-broad pattern; the .gitignore in the workspace does NOT currently ignore yarn.lock, so it must have been filtered upstream in the export tooling); (2) the Dockerfile hard-required the lockfile via `COPY package.json yarn.lock ./` and `yarn install --frozen-lockfile`. Buildx failed at the COPY step because it could not compute a checksum for the missing `/yarn.lock`. Mitigation applied and verified in this iteration: `COPY yarn.lock* ./` glob tolerates 0 matches, and removing `--frozen-lockfile` lets `yarn install` resolve from package.json alone. Confirmed by running `yarn install` in a temp dir containing only package.json — exit 0, node_modules populated. Recommend the user also ensures yarn.lock is actually committed on the next push (check that Emergent's export does not filter it) so multi-arch builds stay reproducible.
